content.body: Homeostasis is the maintenance of a constant internal environment despite changes in external conditions. The body must keep conditions within narrow limits for enzymes to work properly, cells to function, and chemical reactions to proceed at the correct rate.

Conditions regulated by homeostasis:
• Core body temperature: ~37°C (humans)
• Blood glucose concentration: ~80-120 mg/100mL
• Blood pH: ~7.4
• Water and ion balance (osmoregulation)
• CO₂ concentration in blood

Negative feedback: the mechanism of homeostasis
Deviation detected → corrective response → returns to set point
(Negative because the response opposes the change)

content.title: Thermoregulation — Body Temperature Control
content.body:
OVERHEATING responses (lower temperature):
• Sweating: evaporation of sweat cools skin
• Vasodilation: blood vessels in skin dilate → more blood to skin → more heat radiated
• Reduced metabolic rate
• Behaviour: moving to shade, removing clothing

COLD responses (raise temperature):
• Shivering: rapid muscle contractions generate heat
• Vasoconstriction: blood vessels in skin constrict → less blood to surface → less heat lost
• Erector pili muscles raise hairs → traps air insulation (more effective in animals with fur)
• Increased metabolic rate
• Behaviour: moving to warmth, adding clothing

Blood Glucose Regulation — Pancreatic hormones:
After eating (high blood glucose):
1. β-cells in islets of Langerhans detect high glucose
2. β-cells secrete INSULIN
3. Insulin stimulates cells to take up glucose
4. Liver converts glucose → glycogen (glycogenesis)
5. Blood glucose returns to normal

During fasting/exercise (low blood glucose):
1. α-cells detect low glucose
2. α-cells secrete GLUCAGON
3. Glucagon stimulates liver to convert glycogen → glucose (glycogenolysis)
4. Blood glucose rises back to normal

Diabetes Mellitus:
Type 1 (Insulin-dependent): β-cells destroyed (autoimmune). Body produces no insulin. Blood glucose rises dangerously. Treatment: insulin injections, blood glucose monitoring.

Type 2 (Non-insulin-dependent): cells become resistant to insulin (often linked to obesity, diet). Treatment: diet, exercise, oral medication. More common — increasingly affecting young people in Uganda and globally.

questions.question: Homeostasis maintains a constant:
questions.explanation: Homeostasis is the maintenance of a stable internal environment — keeping conditions like temperature and glucose within narrow limits.

questions.question: Negative feedback in homeostasis means:
options: The response makes the deviation worse
The response opposes the change and returns conditions to normal
questions.answer: The response opposes the change and returns conditions to normal
questions.explanation: Negative feedback: the response is in the OPPOSITE direction to the change — it corrects the deviation and restores the set point.

questions.question: In hot conditions, blood vessels in the skin:
questions.explanation: Vasodilation brings more blood to the skin surface, increasing heat radiation and cooling the body.

questions.question: Insulin is produced by:
questions.explanation: Beta (β) cells in the islets of Langerhans in the pancreas produce insulin when blood glucose rises.

questions.question: Insulin causes blood glucose to:
questions.explanation: Insulin stimulates cells to absorb glucose from blood and liver to store it as glycogen — lowering blood glucose.

questions.question: Glucagon is secreted when blood glucose is:
questions.explanation: Glucagon (from α-cells) is released when blood glucose falls too low — it stimulates glycogen breakdown to release glucose.

questions.question: Type 1 diabetes is caused by:
options: Autoimmune destruction of β-cells — no insulin produced
questions.answer: Autoimmune destruction of β-cells — no insulin produced
questions.explanation: In Type 1 diabetes, the immune system destroys the β-cells. The pancreas cannot produce insulin.

questions.question: Shivering in cold conditions generates heat by:
questions.explanation: Shivering is rapid, involuntary muscle contraction that generates metabolic heat to warm the body.

questions.question: Glycogenesis is:
questions.explanation: Glycogenesis: glucose → glycogen. Triggered by insulin when blood glucose is high. Glycogen is stored in the liver and muscles.

questions.question: Why is tight blood glucose control important?
options: Enzymes only work properly within a narrow glucose range
Blood needs exactly 80mg/100mL to be red
questions.answer: Enzymes only work properly within a narrow glucose range
questions.explanation: All cellular reactions are enzyme-controlled. Enzymes have optimal conditions. Extreme blood glucose disrupts osmosis, cell function, and eventually causes organ damage.

content.body: Reproduction is the production of new individuals from existing ones, ensuring the continuation of the species. Sexual reproduction (in humans) involves the fusion of male and female gametes (sex cells) — sperm and egg — in fertilisation to form a zygote.

Male reproductive system:
Testes: produce sperm (by meiosis) and testosterone
Epididymis: sperm mature and are stored
Vas deferens: tube carrying sperm
Seminal vesicle + prostate: add fluid → semen
Urethra + penis: deliver semen

Female reproductive system:
Ovaries: produce eggs (by meiosis) and oestrogen/progesterone
Fallopian tubes (oviducts): egg transport; site of fertilisation
Uterus (womb): site of implantation and fetal development
Cervix: lower part of uterus
Vagina: birth canal

The Menstrual Cycle (28 days):
Days 1-5: Menstruation (uterus lining shed)
Days 6-13: Follicular phase — FSH stimulates follicle development; oestrogen rises → lining thickens
Day 14: Ovulation — LH surge triggers egg release from ovary
Days 15-28: Luteal phase — corpus luteum produces progesterone → lining maintained
If no fertilisation: progesterone falls → lining shed → menstruation

content.title: Fertilisation and Development
content.body:
1. Sperm deposited in vagina, swim through cervix, uterus
2. Fertilisation occurs in fallopian tube: sperm fuses with egg → zygote
3. Zygote undergoes mitosis (cell division) as it travels to uterus
4. Implantation: blastocyst embeds in uterine wall (~day 6-10)
5. Placenta develops: supplies nutrients and O₂, removes CO₂ and waste from fetus
6. Amniotic fluid: surrounds and protects fetus
7. Umbilical cord: connects fetus to placenta
8. Birth ~9 months after fertilisation

Contraception methods:
Hormonal: pill (oestrogen/progesterone), injections, implant — prevent ovulation
Barrier: condom (also prevents STIs), diaphragm
Intrauterine: IUD — prevents implantation
Natural: calendar/rhythm method (least reliable)

Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs) common in Uganda:
HIV/AIDS: virus attacking immune system (CD4 T-cells). No cure but ARVs allow normal life.
Gonorrhoea: bacterial — treated with antibiotics
Syphilis: bacterial — treated with penicillin
HPV: virus — can cause cervical cancer; vaccine available
Prevention: abstinence, mutual faithfulness, condom use (ABC approach)

questions.question: Where does fertilisation normally occur?
questions.explanation: Fertilisation (sperm + egg fusion) normally occurs in the fallopian tube (oviduct), not the uterus.

questions.question: Ovulation occurs on approximately which day of a 28-day cycle?
questions.explanation: Ovulation (egg release from ovary) is triggered by an LH surge around day 14 of a 28-day cycle.

questions.question: The placenta functions to:
options: Supply the fetus with nutrients and O₂, remove waste
questions.answer: Supply the fetus with nutrients and O₂, remove waste
questions.explanation: The placenta allows exchange between maternal and fetal blood — O₂ and nutrients enter fetal blood; CO₂ and wastes leave.

questions.question: Which hormone triggers ovulation?
questions.explanation: A surge in LH (luteinising hormone) around day 14 triggers the follicle to rupture and release the egg — ovulation.

questions.question: Progesterone in the luteal phase maintains:
questions.explanation: Progesterone (from corpus luteum) maintains the thickened uterine lining, ready for implantation. If no fertilisation, progesterone falls and menstruation begins.

questions.question: What is the zygote?
questions.explanation: A zygote is the diploid cell formed when a sperm (n) fuses with an egg (n) during fertilisation. It contains 46 chromosomes.

questions.question: Condoms are the only contraception that also:
questions.explanation: Condoms provide a physical barrier against both pregnancy and sexually transmitted infections (STIs).

questions.question: HIV primarily attacks which cells?
questions.explanation: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) specifically targets and destroys CD4 T-helper cells, progressively weakening the immune system.

questions.question: Sperm are produced in the:
questions.explanation: Sperm are produced in the testes (by meiosis in seminiferous tubules). They then mature in the epididymis.

questions.question: What does FSH do in the female cycle?
questions.explanation: FSH (Follicle Stimulating Hormone) from the pituitary gland stimulates a follicle in the ovary to mature and produce oestrogen.
